title: Intrinsic Pointer Basis and Irreversible Classicality from Coherence Contraction
abstract: The irreversible emergence of classical behavior from a reduced quantum description via a canonical intrinsic decomposition of the density operator is analyzed. In the intrinsic reference basis (IRB), defined for a fixed physical conjugation K (determined by measurement convention, system symmetry, or secular approximation) by diagonalizing the real symmetric part of the state, the density operator separates into a diagonal population sector and a real antisymmetric coherence sector. For the class of Markovian open-system dynamics whose Lindblad operators are diagonal in the IRB, we prove that the quadratic coherence functional is a Lyapunov functional under pure-dephasing or interaction-picture evolution, with each intrinsic coherence component decaying exponentially at a computable rate. This yields a canonical state-dependent operational classicality criterion via the normalized cohesion index, an explicit logarithmic classicalization time controlled by the slowest dephasing rate, and a demonstration that the IRB projectors emerge as dynamically stable pointer sectors under IRB-selective evolution. Suppression of intrinsic coherences is exactly equivalent to suppression of fringe visibility in the corresponding interferometric sector; for a balanced two-path setup the cohesion index coincides with the fringe visibility, making the classicality criterion directly testable with standard interferometric equipment. The approach complements environment-induced einselection: it is applicable whenever a coarse-grained reduced description is available, independently of whether the microscopic system-environment coupling is known.
summary: This paper proposes a new framework for understanding the quantum-to-classical transition by defining a basis (the IRB) directly from the state's own structure. It shows that for certain types of noise, quantum coherences decay predictably, leading to an irreversible emergence of classical behavior. The approach is unique because it does not require knowing the specific details of the environment. Crucially, the paper links this theoretical decay to measurable quantities like fringe visibility in interferometry, making the theory directly testable.
main_problem: The paper addresses the problem of identifying a canonical, model-independent pointer basis and quantifying the emergence of classicality from quantum dynamics when the microscopic system-environment coupling is unknown.
main_result: The author proves that an Intrinsic Reference Basis (IRB) emerges as a dynamically stable pointer sector under IRB-selective dynamics, providing a state-dependent criterion for classicality via a cohesion index.
method: The study uses a mathematical framework involving an antiunitary involution (K) to decompose the density operator into population and coherence sectors, employing Lyapunov analysis and majorization theory to prove monotonic coherence decay.
model_or_system: The framework applies to reduced quantum descriptions of open systems undergoing Markovian GKSL (Lindblad) dynamics, specifically focusing on the 'IRB-selective' regime where Lindblad operators are diagonal in the intrinsic basis.
key_observables: Cohesion index (Pc), fringe visibility (V), quadratic intrinsic coherence (U), and the thermodynamic arrow-of-time proxy (A_lambda).
important_parameters: Classicalization time (t_cl), minimum dephasing rate (Gamma_min), and experimental resolution/tolerance (epsilon).
main_assumptions: Assumes IRB-selective dynamics (Lindblad operators are diagonal in the IRB) and requires a fixed physical conjugation K determined by symmetry or measurement convention.
figures_summary: The notes mention Figure 2(a) but do not provide a full description of the figure contents.
paper_structure: The paper defines the IRB decomposition, proves coherence contraction under specific dynamics, derives operational classicality criteria and timescales, and connects these results to experimental interferometric measurements.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, model-independent way to certify classicality and identify pointer states directly from experimental data (like tomography), which is highly relevant for characterizing noise and decoherence in qubits and other open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
